context_update.summary: The Introduction chunk reviews mechanical property trends of polycrystalline Fe–Al alloys with Al contents from 0 to ~40 at.%. All test materials were as-cast, thermally relaxed, coarse grained, and free of solute/precipitates; alloys near 25–30% Al were slow cooled to give good B2 and moderate DO3 order. Room-temperature yield stress, ductility, and work hardening rate (secant over 1–1.5% strain) are presented: yield stress rises to 25% Al, drops at 25–28%, and plateaus; ductility inversely follows; work hardening rate is low below 20% Al, peaks sharply at 28–29% Al (26–29% range), and declines above 35% Al. The peak is tentatively attributed to core/Peierls effects rather than elastic anisotropy. Dislocation structures change with Al content from single dislocations to superdislocation dipoles with straight screws and sessile products. APB energies (APBI, APBII from Fig. 3) and elastic constants (Zener anisotropy from Fig. 4) are provided as background. Subsequent chunks will present additional mechanical data and TEM dislocation analysis to clarify the work hardening peak mechanism.
sample_aliases: Fe–x%Al where x is Al content in at.%
processing_route: as-cast and thermally relaxed, coarse grain size
slow cooled to obtain B2 order with moderate DO3 order
key_variables: plastic strain range for work hardening (1–1.5%)
microstructure: single (1/2)<111> dislocations in disordered state
two or four (1/2)<111> partial superdislocations in B2 or DO3 order
straight screw dislocations
sessile dislocation segments
superdislocation dipoles
properties: yield stress vs. Al content (steady increase, drop at 25–28%, plateau)
ductility vs. Al content (inverse to yield stress, slight maximum at 28% Al)
work hardening rate vs. Al content (sharp peak at 26–30% Al, low below 20%, moderate above 35%)
mechanisms: dipole hardening: superdislocation dipoles strengthen more than single dislocation dipoles
core/Peierls effects responsible for work hardening peak rather than direct elastic anisotropy
APB energies influence partial dislocation separation
figures_or_tables: Fig. 1 (yield stress and ductility vs. Al content)
Fig. 2 (work hardening rate vs. Al content)
Fig. 3 (APB energies in B2 and DO3)
Fig. 4 (elastic constants and Zener anisotropy)

Sample aliases: Fe–x%Al where x is Al content in at.%; Fe–0.1%B (boron-containing alloys)

Mechanisms: dipole hardening: superdislocation dipoles strengthen more than single dislocation dipoles; core/Peierls effects responsible for work hardening peak rather than direct elastic anisotropy; APB energies influence partial dislocation separation

context_update.summary: The current chunk completes the Experimental section and presents Results through Section 3.2. Experimental details are given: six binary Fe–Al alloys (15, 20, 25, 28, 30, 35 at.% Al) and two boron-containing variants (20%Al–0.1B, 30%Al–0.1B) were drop-cast into a 25 mm Cu mould, producing ~0.25 mm grain size; slow cooling ensured B2 order and, for 25–30% Al, a good degree of DO3 order. Compression tests were performed at room temperature at 3×10⁻⁴ s⁻¹ to 2–3% strain with the axis parallel to the cast cylinder; stress–strain curves showed no discrete yield and were slightly curved. Mechanical data confirm the work hardening peak lies in the 26–29% Al range (maximum at 29% Al) and that boron additions slightly raise flow stress without notably altering work hardening rate. Detailed TEM observations of dislocation structures as a function of Al content are presented: single (1/2)⟨111⟩ dislocations with cellular/dipole arrangements in 15–20% Al; loosely coupled superdislocation partials and dipoles in DO₃‑ordered 25% Al; straight near‑screw superdislocations trailing APBII faults and forming sessile ⟨100⟩ locks via reactions in 28% Al (the peak‑hardening composition); superdislocations with narrow partial separation and dipole-dominated structures in 30–35% Al, accompanied by remnant APBII fringes from DO₃ order destruction. Figures 5–19 supply the TEM evidence, while earlier Figs. 1,2,4 are referenced. Subsequent chunks can build on these processing–microstructure relations to discuss the hardening mechanism in full.
processing_route: induction melting and drop casting into 25 mm Cu mould
slow cooling → high B2 order, moderate DO3 order for 25–30% Al
spark-machined compression samples (3 mm dia. or 5×5 mm², height : width = 2:1)
room‑temperature compression to 2–3% strain at 3×10⁻⁴ s⁻¹
compression axis parallel to cast cylinder axis
TEM foils cut perpendicular to stress axis, electropolished with 25% HNO₃ in methanol at –30°C
microstructure: Fe–15%Al: single (1/2)⟨111⟩ dislocations, loose cellular dipoles (Fig. 5)
Fe–20%Al: curved single dislocations, screw/edge dipoles and loops (Figs. 6,7)
Fe–25%Al (DO3): straight near‑screw dislocations, loose superdislocation partials, loosely coupled screw dipoles, APBI-fault trailing (Figs. 8,9)
Fe–28%Al: straight near‑screw superdislocations (two (1/2)⟨111⟩ partials) trailing APBII faults, no APBI faults; dislocation reactions form sessile ⟨100⟩ edge locks on cube planes (Figs.10–13)
Fe–30%Al: superdislocations with small partial separation, straight screw and edge dipoles, remnant APBII fringes from weak DO3 destruction (Figs.14–16)
Fe–35%Al: B2 superdislocations with close partials, screw/edge dipoles pulled from obstacles, weak APBII fringes (Figs.17–19)
properties: work hardening rate peak in 26–29% Al (max. at 29% Al) over 1–1.5% strain
boron addition: slight flow‑stress increase, negligible change in work hardening rate
no discrete yield point; slightly curved stress–strain curves
mechanisms: APB‑energy‑controlled coupling of superdislocation partials (low in 25% Al, increasingly tight at higher Al)
peak work hardening in Fe–28%Al linked to formation of straight superdislocation dipoles and sessile ⟨100⟩ locks by glide-plane reactions
dipole dragging and loop formation from gliding screw dislocations
destruction of DO3 order by gliding (1/2)⟨111⟩ partials reveals APBII fault contrast

context_update.summary: This chunk completes the TEM description of Fe-35% Al dislocation structures (Figs. 17–19), showing B2 superdislocations with closely spaced (1/2)⟨111⟩ partials, abundant edge dipole segments, and weak remnant fringes indicating DO3 order destruction. The Discussion begins by confirming mechanical property trends: flow stress peaks at 25% Al, a small ductility peak appears near 28% Al, and work hardening rate shows a sharp maximum near 28% Al for ordered alloys. A model is developed where work hardening arises primarily from dipole debris left by mobile screw dislocations; ordered alloys harden more due to superdislocation dipoles, while 25% Al exhibits lower hardening because APB energies fail to couple dislocations. Disordered alloys (15–20% Al) show low hardening due to extensive edge dislocation content and fewer dipoles. The roles of elastic anisotropy and Peierls forces are discussed: elastic anisotropy differences are too small to explain the 28% Al peak, whereas high Peierls forces favoring screw orientation and facilitating dipole pinning are proposed as the likely origin. Additionally, ⟨111⟩ dislocation reactions produce ⟨100⟩ edge dislocations that act as strong, Peierls-stabilized obstacles. The small ductility peak is correlated with the work hardening peak via the Considère criterion. Later chunks should explore further the Peierls mechanism and core structure effects.

context_update.summary: The current chunk addresses the discussion of a small ductility peak at ~28% Al in Fe–Al alloys, linking it via the Considère criterion to the high work hardening rate and relatively constant flow stress in the 26–30% Al range. The Summary section recaps key findings: work hardening was studied across 15–35% Al covering disordered, DO3, and B2 ordered states; the work hardening rate is higher in ordered alloys and peaks sharply near Fe–28% Al, coinciding with a ductility maximum and a fall in flow stress above 25% Al. Dislocation structures evolve from single bowed dislocations (15–20% Al) to paired superdislocations with straight screw segments (28–35% Al). DO3 Fe–25% Al lacks superdislocations, trailing APB. No four-partial superdislocations were observed. Dipoles are abundant—single dislocation dipoles in disordered, superdislocation dipoles in ordered materials. Two factors promote high work hardening: superdislocation dipole locking and a tendency for screw orientation, which is attributed to strong Peierls forces near 25–30% Al rather than elastic anisotropy. Additionally, <111> dislocation reactions forming sessile <100> edge dislocations near 28–30% Al may contribute. The peak in ductility is explained by the high work hardening rate. Subsequent chunks should retain the correlation between composition (especially 28% Al), dislocation configurations, and work hardening mechanisms, particularly the role of Peierls forces and superdislocation dipoles.
microstructure: single bowed dislocations in disordered 15–20% Al
paired superdislocations with straight screw segments in highly ordered 28–35% Al
no superdislocations in DO3 Fe–25% Al, single dislocations trailing APB
no four-partial superdislocations observed
dipoles: single dislocation dipoles in disordered, superdislocation dipoles in ordered
sessile <100> edge dislocations from <111> reactions near 28–30% Al
properties: work hardening rate peak near 28% Al
higher work hardening rate in ordered state
flow stress falls above 25% Al, relatively constant in 26–30% Al
mechanisms: Considère criterion linking ductility to work hardening rate
superdislocation dipole formation for stronger obstacle locks
increased screw orientation tendency due to Peierls forces at 25–30% Al (not elastic anisotropy)
dislocation reactions forming sessile <100> edge dislocations

Material systems: Fe–Al polycrystalline alloys (0–~40 at.% Al); disordered Fe–Al (<20% Al); B2-ordered Fe–Al; DO3-ordered Fe–Al (25–30% Al); Fe–15%Al; Fe–20%Al; Fe–25%Al (DO3 ordered); Fe–28%Al (peak work hardening)
Sample aliases: Fe–x%Al where x is Al content in at.%; Fe–0.1%B (boron-containing alloys); Fe–15%Al; Fe–20%Al; Fe–25%Al; Fe–28%Al; Fe–30%Al; Fe–35%Al
Processing route: as-cast and thermally relaxed, coarse grain size; slow cooled to obtain B2 order with moderate DO3 order; no solute atoms or precipitates; induction melting and drop casting into 25 mm Cu mould; grain size ~0.25 mm; slow cooling → high B2 order, moderate DO3 order for 25–30% Al; spark-machined compression samples (3 mm dia. or 5×5 mm², height : width = 2:1); room‑temperature compression to 2–3% strain at 3×10⁻⁴ s⁻¹
Key variables: Al content (at.%); state of order (disordered, B2, DO3); plastic strain range for work hardening (1–1.5%); room temperature testing; Al content (15, 20, 25, 28, 30, 35 at.%); B addition (0.1 at.%); ordered state (disordered, B2, DO3); plastic strain (2–3% total)
Microstructure: single (1/2)<111> dislocations in disordered state; two or four (1/2)<111> partial superdislocations in B2 or DO3 order; straight screw dislocations; sessile dislocation segments; superdislocation dipoles; Fe–15%Al: single (1/2)⟨111⟩ dislocations, loose cellular dipoles (Fig. 5); Fe–20%Al: curved single dislocations, screw/edge dipoles and loops (Figs. 6,7); Fe–25%Al (DO3): straight near‑screw dislocations, loose superdislocation partials, loosely coupled screw dipoles, APBI-fault trailing (Figs. 8,9)
